free_text: Numbers of Prompt Neutrons per Fission for U233, U235,
Pu239 and Cf252
Large cadmium-loaded liquid scintillator
and fission counter.
In order to deduced the mean number of
neutrons per fission from the observed number of
pulses per gate, it is necessary to know the
efficiency of the detector for fission neutrons. The
efficiency is determined in two ways. The first is to
assume the accurately known value of nubar for 235U
at thermal energy. The detector efficiency is the
mean number of neutrons detected per gate divided by
the known value of nubar. Another method is to
replace the fission detector with proton recoil
neutron detector such as hydrogen-filled proportional
counter that is inserted into the center of liquid
scintillator in place of the fission counter. Then
monoenergetic neutrons are passed through the
proportional counter, and the gate opened with pulses
produced by recoil protons. Every proportional
counter pulse is associated with the scattering of a
neutron out of the beam and into a liquid
scintillator by a hydrogen nucleus in the counter.
For fission spectrum, about 98.5% of the neutrons are
captured in the detector.
The standard deviations reflect
statistical uncertainty due to the number of fission
events observed and to
the uncertainty in the nubar of 235U.
BP

The measurement of nubar for neutron-induced fission
of 233U, 235U and 239Pu relative to the nubar for the
spontaneous fission of 252Cf. Between -6.28 MeV and
thermal energy, zero threshold reaction. This
interpretation was questioned by the CF-252
evaluators (Denise Neudecker). Additional comment
from Naohiko: "...the authors provide the Pu240 with
En=-6.28 MeV rather than En='Spont.' in Table I.
(What is -6.28 MeV? Neutron separation energy of
240Pu, which is currently known to be ~6.53 MeV.) ."
